AI.method: Built around five pillars: (1) goal-scoped scientific decision governance, (2) translate-then-reason for multimodal input, (3) evidence governance for auditable traceability, (4) collaborative team science, (5) real-world application scenarios. System combines thin interaction layer, contextual research patterns, Agent Runtime and Workflow Engine, Evidence-DAG audit sidecar, and Scientific Model Router.
AI.result: Developed SciForge as an open-source desktop application with mobile supervision support. Demonstrated practical impact through eight end-to-end user cases, including multi-day agentic research sprints for gene discovery, AI-guided de novo protein design, molecular optimization, and genome-to-BGC discovery.
AI.conclusion: SciForge provides a comprehensive AI workbench that addresses the fragmentation of scientific artifacts while maintaining auditability and collaborative governance. It represents a significant step toward more integrated, traceable, and practical AI-assisted scientific research environments.

title: Hard Rules, Soft Preferences: Bridging Reasoning, Learning, and Optimization for Personalized Packing Checklist Generation
summary: Packing for air travel is recurring and error-prone: the checklist must be personal and context-aware, yet feasible under safety rules, item dependencies, and luggage limits. Existing packing assistants are template-driven and generic, or recommendation-driven but unconstrained, leaving users to manually patch regulatory and capacity violations. We propose a reasoning-guided learning framework with three stages: (1) a symbolic engine that generates a regulation-aware seed checklist with explicit dependency structure, (2) a two-stage preference learner that estimates inclusion and priority utilities from user add and remove actions while mitigating survivorship bias, and (3) a CP-SAT optimizer that selects a compact, compliant subset. The architecture instantiates a general pattern for constrained personalization, applicable wherever hard feasibility coexists with sparse preference signals. On 604 labeled trip scenarios, comprising 29K inclusion labels and 343K pairwise comparisons, the symbolic engine attains 99.7% recall and 0.96 rubric validity, compared with 0.78 to 0.81 for frontier LLMs. Gradient-boosted trees and LambdaMART reach an AUC-ROC of 0.943 and an NDCG@5 of 0.923. CP-SAT attains 100% constraint satisfaction, compared with 28% for greedy selection and 10% for random selection. Deployment in FlyEnJoy, a production iOS travel app, doubled checklist completions and reduced editing and completion time.

AI.tldr: A reasoning-guided learning framework for air travel packing that combines symbolic rules with preference learning to generate personalized, regulation-compliant checklists that outperform LLMs and other baselines.
AI.motivation: Current packing assistants are either generic templates or unconstrained recommendations, forcing users to manually fix regulatory violations and capacity issues. There's a need for personalized, context-aware checklists that comply with safety rules, handle item dependencies, and respect luggage limits.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) symbolic engine generates regulation-aware seed checklists with dependency structure, (2) two-stage preference learner estimates inclusion and priority utilities from user actions while mitigating survivorship bias, (3) CP-SAT optimizer selects compact, compliant subsets.
AI.result: On 604 trip scenarios with 29K inclusion labels and 343K pairwise comparisons: symbolic engine achieves 99.7% recall and 0.96 rubric validity (vs LLMs: 0.78-0.81); ML models reach AUC-ROC 0.943 and NDCG@5 0.923; CP-SAT achieves 100% constraint satisfaction (vs greedy: 28%, random: 10%); deployment in FlyEnJoy iOS app doubled checklist completions and reduced editing/completion time.
AI.conclusion: The reasoning-guided learning framework effectively addresses constrained personalization problems where hard feasibility requirements coexist with sparse preference signals, outperforming existing approaches and showing practical benefits in real-world deployment.

title: Harmonizing AI Safety Thresholds
summary: Frontier AI companies have published capability thresholds that differ substantially, making it difficult for third parties to verify whether a threshold has been crossed or to compare requirements across companies. Moreover, without common minimum thresholds, risk mitigation may be inconsistent, creating a potential race to the bottom in safety standards. We develop a methodology for deriving harmonized thresholds across three risk domains. For misuse risks (cyber and biological), we take expected harm as the key primitive and use an explicit risk-modeling approach that accounts for risk channels and model release conditions. For automated AI R&D, we base our proposed threshold on the observed rate of AI progress rather than expected harm. Our analysis expands upon prior work and highlights existing empirical gaps and limitations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a methodology for creating harmonized AI capability thresholds across companies to enable verification, comparison, and consistent risk mitigation, addressing current divergent standards.
AI.motivation: Current AI companies use substantially different capability thresholds, making third-party verification difficult, preventing meaningful comparisons across companies, and potentially creating a race to the bottom in safety standards due to inconsistent risk mitigation.
AI.method: Develops a methodology for deriving harmonized thresholds across three risk domains: 1) For misuse risks (cyber and biological), uses expected harm as key primitive with explicit risk modeling accounting for risk channels and model release conditions. 2) For automated AI R&D, bases thresholds on observed rate of AI progress rather than expected harm.
AI.result: The analysis expands on prior work and identifies existing empirical gaps and limitations in current approaches to AI capability threshold setting.
AI.conclusion: A harmonized approach to AI capability thresholds is needed to enable verification, comparison, and consistent risk mitigation across companies, addressing the current fragmented landscape that hampers safety oversight.

title: Information-Directed Sampling for Causal Bandits
summary: Causal bandits exploit structural relationships among variables to share information across interventions and accelerate the identification of high-reward decisions. In many applications, however, some variables cannot be directly manipulated, even though they influence the reward and provide useful information about the underlying causal system. We study contextual causal bandits with non-manipulable variables, where context variables are observed before action selection and additional variables are observed after each intervention. Assuming a known causal graph without latent confounding, we adopt a Bayesian formulation in which the conditional probability tables of the observational distribution constitute the unknown parameter. This representation allows observations collected under one intervention to update reward estimates for other interventions through their shared causal mechanisms. We develop causal variants of Thompson Sampling and Information-Directed Sampling (IDS) for this setting. For Thompson Sampling, we establish an entropy-dependent sublinear Bayesian regret bound. For IDS, we derive an entropy-dependent regret bound that explicitly quantifies the additional error introduced by Monte Carlo approximation of the expected regret and information gain; when these quantities are available exactly, the bound recovers the standard sublinear IDS rate. We further provide high-probability confidence bounds for the Monte Carlo estimates used by the algorithm. Experiments on several synthetic causal bandit tasks show that the proposed methods outperform causal and non-causal baselines by more effectively exploiting information shared across interventions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents causal bandit algorithms for settings with non-manipulable context variables, developing Bayesian methods that exploit causal structure to share information across interventions.
AI.motivation: Many real-world applications have variables that cannot be directly manipulated but still influence rewards and provide useful causal information. Current causal bandit methods don't adequately handle such non-manipulable variables, creating a gap between theory and practical applications.
AI.method: The authors adopt a Bayesian formulation where conditional probability tables of observational distributions are unknown parameters. They develop causal variants of Thompson Sampling and Information-Directed Sampling (IDS) that handle non-manipulable context variables, with Monte Carlo approximations for practical implementation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows: 1) entropy-dependent sublinear Bayesian regret bound for Thompson Sampling, 2) similar regret bound for IDS with explicit quantification of Monte Carlo approximation error, 3) high-probability confidence bounds for Monte Carlo estimates. Experiments on synthetic causal bandit tasks demonstrate the methods outperform both causal and non-causal baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed causal bandit algorithms effectively exploit shared causal information across interventions when dealing with non-manipulable variables, bridging theoretical causal bandits with practical applications where not all variables can be manipulated.

title: CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data
summary: Evaluations should do more than measure a models current performance. They should tell us what to fix for the next model iteration and provide a way to generate targeted post training data. Most evaluation pipelines identify weak examples, topics, or categories, but they leave the underlying capability failure implicit: they say where a model fails, not why. We introduce CRAFT, a method that converts any rubric based evaluation dataset into a model specific diagnosis of weak capabilities. CRAFT treats each grading criterion as a capability probe: it extracts a capability description from every prompt rubric pair, clusters these descriptions into a hierarchical capability tree, scores the target model at every node, and selects low performing nodes dynamically across tree levels, at the granularity where each failure is clearest. The selected weak capabilities then direct the generation of targeted supervised finetuning data. Holding the data generation, finetuning, and evaluation setup fixed, we compare CRAFT against prompt level EvalTree clustering and untargeted random generation on four open source models, two professional domains (finance and legal), and 13 held out benchmarks disjoint from the diagnostic data. CRAFT achieves the strongest finance domain average for all four models under repeated temperature decoding; on legal domain, it is strongest for three of four models and remains within the decoding variance bands of the best baseline on the fourth. Diagnosing weaknesses at the level of rubric criteria, rather than prompts or categories, thus yields both a sharper picture of what a model cannot do and measurably better models after finetuning on that diagnosis.

AI.tldr: CRAFT converts evaluation rubrics into capability diagnoses to identify why models fail and generate targeted training data, improving model performance through focused finetuning.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations only identify where models fail (weak examples/topics/categories) but don't explain why they fail, leaving underlying capability failures implicit. There's a need for diagnostic tools that reveal specific weak capabilities to guide targeted improvements.
AI.method: CRAFT treats each evaluation rubric criterion as a capability probe: extracts capability descriptions from prompt-rubric pairs, clusters them into hierarchical capability trees, scores models at every node, and selects low-performing nodes across tree levels at optimal granularity where failures are clearest. Weak capabilities then guide targeted supervised finetuning data generation.
AI.result: On four open-source models across finance and legal domains with 13 held-out benchmarks, CRAFT achieved strongest finance domain average for all models under repeated temperature decoding. In legal domain, it was strongest for 3 of 4 models and remained within decoding variance bands of the best baseline on the fourth.
AI.conclusion: Diagnosing weaknesses at rubric criterion level (vs. prompt or category level) yields both clearer understanding of model limitations and measurably better models after finetuning based on that diagnosis, making evaluations more actionable for iterative improvement.

title: Rethinking Transfer in Continual Learning: A Replay-Based Realisation
summary: Continual learning studies how deployed language models can continually acquire new tasks without expensive retraining from scratch. Existing methods, whether rehearsal-based (replaying stored past data) or rehearsal-free (regularising or isolating parameters), overwhelmingly target one objective: preventing catastrophic forgetting. Forward transfer, the past helping the future, has meanwhile been pursued almost exclusively through parameter reuse, with no explicit account of when transfer should be expected at all. We begin one step earlier: before designing a transfer mechanism, we ask when transfer should exist at all. We answer with a framework of three measurable conditions: the target task must leave room for improvement beyond its own limited supervision, transferable information must survive continued optimisation, and replay must come from compatible previous tasks. We instantiate this view as Transfer-Selective Replay (TSR), which selects replay data predicted to benefit the incoming task rather than replaying past examples indiscriminately. Selection is guided by a zero-training task signature, while distillation preserves stability on previous tasks. Under the standard continual learning protocol in the low-budget regime, TSR consistently improves forward transfer while maintaining stability, outperforming existing replay baselines across heterogeneous and homogeneous task streams. More broadly, the results argue for treating transfer as a first-class objective of continual learning, to be understood before it is engineered.

AI.tldr: TSR is a continual learning method that selectively replays past data predicted to help new tasks rather than replaying indiscriminately, improving forward transfer while maintaining stability.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning methods focus almost exclusively on preventing catastrophic forgetting, with forward transfer pursued mainly through parameter reuse without understanding when transfer should actually occur. There's a need to first understand when transfer should exist before designing transfer mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors propose Transfer-Selective Replay (TSR), which selects replay data predicted to benefit incoming tasks using zero-training task signatures. The method is guided by a framework of three measurable conditions for transfer: tasks must have room for improvement, transferable information must survive optimization, and replay must come from compatible previous tasks.
AI.result: TSR consistently improves forward transfer while maintaining stability under standard continual learning protocols in low-budget regimes. It outperforms existing replay baselines across both heterogeneous and homogeneous task streams.
AI.conclusion: Forward transfer should be treated as a first-class objective in continual learning that needs to be understood before it can be effectively engineered. The framework provides measurable conditions for when transfer should occur, and TSR demonstrates practical benefits of this approach.

title: Field-Aware RankMixer with Dual-Stream Bilinear Fusion for the Tencent UNI-REC Challenge
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, KDD Cup 2026 Tencent UNIREC Challenge
summary: This paper presents our solution to the KDD Cup 2026 Tencent UNIREC Challenge. The task requires joint modeling of multi-domain user behavior sequences and non-sequential multi-field features for target-ad pCVR prediction. We develop a Field-Aware RankMixer (FA-RankMixer) with dual-stream bilinear fusion. The model first applies target-aware DIN modules to extract user interests from multiple behavior domains. It also models recent and earlier interests separately for the longest behavior sequence. The model then forms semantic tokens based on feature fields and behavior domains and uses RankMixer blocks for cross-token interaction. A shallow MLP stream complements the deep RankMixer stream, and a group-wise bilinear module fuses their representations. Our final solution ranks ninth on the official leaderboard. Our code is available at https://github.com/PixelCookie-zyf/TAAC-2026-SeRankMixer.

AI.tldr: Team develops FA-RankMixer model with dual-stream bilinear fusion for multi-domain user behavior sequence modeling and non-sequential feature modeling in CTR prediction, achieving 9th place in KDD Cup 2026 competition.
AI.motivation: The challenge requires joint modeling of multi-domain user behavior sequences and non-sequential multi-field features for target-ad pCVR prediction in the KDD Cup 2026 Tencent UNIREC Challenge competition.
AI.method: Developed Field-Aware RankMixer (FA-RankMixer) with dual-stream bilinear fusion: uses target-aware DIN modules for multi-domain interest extraction, separates recent/earlier interests for longest sequences, forms semantic tokens based on feature fields and behavior domains, uses RankMixer blocks for cross-token interaction, shallow MLP stream complements deep RankMixer stream, and group-wise bilinear module fuses representations.
AI.result: The final solution ranked ninth on the official KDD Cup 2026 Tencent UNIREC Challenge leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: The FA-RankMixer model with dual-stream bilinear fusion effectively addresses the challenge of joint modeling multi-domain user behaviors and non-sequential features, demonstrating competitive performance with a 9th place finish in the competition.

title: Do Generative Models Keep Time? A Time-Aware Evaluation of Synthetic Sequential Tabular Data
summary: Synthetic sequential tabular data are increasingly used for privacy-preserving data sharing, yet a generator can reproduce every marginal and every foreign-key relationship while emitting timestamps that run backwards or repeat, and while sending entities along paths that no real entity followed. Conventional tabular evaluation, which pools records into static distributions, is blind to such failures. We present a taxonomy-guided evaluation protocol for temporal fidelity, in which the applicable measurements are determined by the data rather than fixed in advance. Each dataset is first characterized along four properties: how time is represented, whether observations are regularly sampled, whether trajectories are mutually dependent, and how the schema links entities to their histories. These properties determine which evaluation dimensions are meaningful. The protocol then measures timestamp validity, cross-sectional structure at aligned time points, within-entity dynamics, and time-varying relational structure, and recasts utility and privacy evaluation over trajectories rather than isolated rows. We apply the protocol to eight generative models across thirteen datasets spanning six domains. Rankings under conventional evaluation disagree substantially with those obtained under temporal evaluation, and the resulting failures are architecture-coherent rather than random. Temporal fidelity must therefore be measured on the time axis itself, rather than inferred from pooled record distributions.

AI.tldr: A new evaluation protocol for temporal fidelity in synthetic sequential tabular data that goes beyond conventional static evaluation by assessing timestamp validity, entity dynamics, and time-varying relationships along four data properties.
AI.motivation: Current synthetic sequential tabular data generators can reproduce marginal distributions and foreign-key relationships while having serious temporal fidelity issues like backward/irregular timestamps and unrealistic entity trajectories. Conventional tabular evaluation methods that pool records into static distributions fail to detect these temporal problems.
AI.method: A taxonomy-guided evaluation protocol that first characterizes datasets along four properties: time representation, regularity of sampling, trajectory dependencies, and schema linking entities to histories. Based on these properties, the protocol measures timestamp validity, cross-sectional structure at aligned times, within-entity dynamics, and time-varying relational structure, while also recasting utility and privacy evaluation over trajectories rather than isolated rows.
AI.result: Applied to eight generative models across thirteen datasets spanning six domains. Rankings under conventional evaluation substantially disagree with those under temporal evaluation, and discovered failures are architecture-coherent rather than random.
AI.conclusion: Temporal fidelity must be measured directly on the time axis itself rather than inferred from pooled record distributions, as conventional static evaluation misses critical temporal problems that affect data quality and utility.

title: ASK-NN: An Asymmetric Nearest-Neighbor Test that detects Distribution Drifts in Natural Language
summary: Hallucinations and artificial text in LLM-generated outputs often appear as distributional deviations between prompt and response hidden-state distributions. Since prompts or retrieved contexts typically serve as reference samples and responses as query samples, with major differences in length, these asymmetries motivate the use of change test statistics that treat the two samples differently. We consider an asymmetric two-sample test ASK-NN based on the directed k-nearest-neighbor graph. Our statistic counts reference points whose nearest neighbor in the pooled sample is also a reference point. Under the permutation null, it admits an exact finite-sample conditional mean and variance; we further establish asymptotic normality and consistency under fixed alternatives. ASK-NN is computationally effective and easy to implement. Empirically, it is competitive with kernel and graph-based baselines on synthetic benchmarks, artificial-text detection, and LLM hallucination detection from token-level hidden states.

AI.tldr: ASK-NN is an asymmetric two-sample test for detecting LLM hallucinations by comparing prompt and response hidden-state distributions using directed k-nearest-neighbor graphs.
AI.motivation: LLM hallucinations and artificial text appear as distributional deviations between prompt (reference) and response (query) hidden-state distributions. Traditional symmetric tests fail due to length asymmetries between prompts and responses, motivating an asymmetric test approach.
AI.method: Proposes ASK-NN, an asymmetric two-sample test based on directed k-nearest-neighbor graphs. The statistic counts reference points whose nearest neighbor in the pooled sample is also a reference point. Provides exact finite-sample conditional mean/variance under permutation null, with asymptotic normality and consistency proofs.
AI.result: ASK-NN is computationally efficient and competitive with kernel and graph-based baselines across synthetic benchmarks, artificial-text detection, and LLM hallucination detection from token-level hidden states.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetric ASK-NN test effectively addresses length asymmetries in prompt-response comparisons for detecting LLM hallucinations and artificial text, offering theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness.

title: Neural Non-Equilibrium Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Corrected Boltzmann Sampling
summary: Sampling from an unnormalized Boltzmann density requires proposals that move probability mass globally while retaining enough path-probability information for statistical correction. We introduce Neural Non-Equilibrium Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (NHMC), a train-then-correct learned Hamiltonian sampler. Starting from a tractable base distribution, NHMC learns stochastic Hamiltonian-style paths toward the target. Once training is complete, the learned proposal parameters are fixed; the proposal then generates complete paths and endpoint configurations, which are statistically corrected using the recorded non-equilibrium work. This dimensionless generalized work is determined by the probability ratio between the forward proposal path and a reverse reference path. During training, minimizing its mean reduces a path-space KL divergence and controls an upper bound on endpoint mismatch. During evaluation, the same quantity defines weights for self-normalized importance sampling on paths (path-SNIS), estimates normalizing constants or free-energy differences, and gives the acceptance ratio for path-space independent Metropolis-Hastings (path-IMH). The same forward-reverse laws also define a shared-bridge round-trip Metropolis kernel that acts directly on configurations and preserves the Boltzmann target. On double-well and finite-volume lattice $φ^4$ targets, the NHMC construction gives corrected estimates when path overlap is sufficient; when overlap is poor, weight degeneracy, low acceptance, and long autocorrelation expose proposal failure. We additionally report a molecular internal-coordinate feasibility study using an MD prior and learned-force path proposal.

AI.tldr: Neural Non-Equilibrium Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (NHMC) is a train-then-correct learned Hamiltonian sampler that uses stochastic Hamiltonian-style paths from a base distribution to target distribution, with statistical correction via non-equilibrium work metrics.
AI.motivation: Sampling from unnormalized Boltzmann densities requires proposals that move probability mass globally while retaining path-probability information for statistical correction. Existing methods may struggle with complex energy landscapes.
AI.method: NHMC learns stochastic Hamiltonian-style paths from tractable base distribution to target. Training minimizes mean non-equilibrium work (path-space KL divergence bound). Evaluation uses path-SNIS (self-normalized importance sampling on paths), path-IMH acceptance ratio, and shared-bridge round-trip Metropolis kernel. The dimensionless generalized work is computed from forward proposal path vs reverse reference path probability ratios.
AI.result: NHMC provides corrected estimates when path overlap is sufficient. On double-well and finite-volume lattice φ⁴ targets, it works well with sufficient overlap, but shows weight degeneracy, low acceptance, and long autocorrelation when overlap is poor. Also demonstrated on molecular internal-coordinate feasibility study using MD prior and learned-force path proposal.
AI.conclusion: Neural NHMC offers a principled learned sampling framework that can handle complex target distributions when path proposals are well-matched, but reveals proposal failures through diagnostic metrics when overlap is insufficient. The method provides both configuration sampling and thermodynamic integration capabilities through the same forward-reverse work formulation.

title: Toward Federated Multimodal Graph Foundation Models: A Topology-Aware Multimodal Alignment Framework
summary: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs), whose nodes carry modalities such as images and text alongside topological structure, now pervade applications including social platforms, e-commerce, and biomedical networks, offering richer semantic signals than single-modality graphs. In practice, such graphs are fragmented across privacy-restricted silos owned by different platforms and institutions, so learning a broadly transferable model over them demands collaborative training that never exposes raw data. This places the task at the intersection of multimodal graph learning and federated learning, yet existing methods cover only one side of it. To address the challenges from these two perspectives, we propose FedGAMMA, casting federated multimodal graph foundation learning as a two-stage semantic-structural alignment problem of federated pre-training and prompt-based fine-tuning. During pre-training, a shared-private semantic enhancer disentangles cross-modal commonality from modality-specific information, aligning it through optimal transport, a topology-aware graph fusion module decouples semantic and structural views via semantic residual graphs and dual positional encodings, and a dual-channel affinity-aware aggregation mechanism estimates client similarity from feature and graph centroids without exposing raw data. During fine-tuning, FedGAMMA adapts the pretrained encoder through lightweight graph-aware prompts, a shared prompt pool with controlled exploration, and channel-wise prompt synchronization. Experiments on twelve multimodal graph datasets show FedGAMMA consistently surpassing a broad range of baselines across downstream tasks, with gains of up to 12.96%. FedGAMMA further outperforms competitive baselines accross multi-domain datasets on multiple tasks with up to 5.71% under few-shot learning scenario.

AI.tldr: FedGAMMA: A federated multimodal graph foundation learning framework using two-stage semantic-structural alignment with federated pre-training and prompt-based fine-tuning for cross-silo multimodal-attributed graphs.
AI.motivation: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) are fragmented across privacy-restricted silos, requiring collaborative training without exposing raw data. Existing methods only address either multimodal graph learning or federated learning, but not both simultaneously.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Federated pre-training with shared-private semantic enhancer (disentangling cross-modal commonality via optimal transport), topology-aware graph fusion (semantic residual graphs and dual positional encodings), and dual-channel affinity-aware aggregation (estimating client similarity from feature/graph centroids); 2) Prompt-based fine-tuning with lightweight graph-aware prompts, shared prompt pool with controlled exploration, and channel-wise prompt synchronization.
AI.result: Outperforms broad range of baselines across 12 multimodal graph datasets with gains up to 12.96%, and achieves up to 5.71% improvement under few-shot learning scenarios across multi-domain datasets on multiple tasks.
AI.conclusion: FedGAMMA effectively addresses the intersection of multimodal graph learning and federated learning, enabling privacy-preserving collaborative training over fragmented multimodal-attributed graphs while achieving strong performance across various downstream tasks.

title: A Benchmark for Electrical Load Forecasting Across Grid Levels: Time-Series Transformers Outperform Established Methods
summary: Accurate load forecasting at multiple grid levels is essential for future smart grids, ranging from aggregated control area forecasts for balancing supply and demand to forecasts of individual end-consumer loads for demand-side management and energy management systems. We present a comprehensive benchmark for load forecasting across grid levels, comprising three datasets that represent a transmission system operator control area, low-voltage grid feeders, and individual end consumers. We evaluate ten methods for short-term load forecasting and find that Transformer-based approaches consistently outperform established methods, reducing forecast error by 6.6-10.7 %. To analyze the impact of architectural design, we introduce YAformer, a flexible Transformer architecture that integrates modifications from prior work and is optimized via hyperparameter optimization. However, the standard Transformer achieves superior performance, suggesting that these architectural modifications are not required for accurate load forecasting. We further evaluate the Transformer-based time-series foundation model Chronos-2, which demonstrates competitive zero-shot performance on two datasets but fails to accurately capture special events in the TSO data. Detailed analyses reveal model-specific strengths and weaknesses, and ablation studies highlight the importance of long input contexts, covariates and continuous retraining - aspects that are often overlooked in the time-series forecasting literature.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based models outperform traditional load forecasting methods across multiple grid levels, with standard Transformers achieving better results than modified architectures, while highlighting the importance of input context, covariates, and continuous retraining.
AI.motivation: Accurate load forecasting at multiple grid levels (from aggregated control areas to individual consumers) is essential for smart grid operations including balancing supply and demand, demand-side management, and energy management systems.
AI.method: Comprehensive benchmark across three grid-level datasets (TSO control area, low-voltage feeders, individual consumers) evaluating ten short-term load forecasting methods. Introduces YAformer (flexible Transformer architecture with modifications) and evaluates standard Transformers plus the Chronos-2 foundation model with hyperparameter optimization.
AI.result: Transformer-based approaches consistently outperform established methods, reducing forecast error by 6.6-10.7%. Standard Transformers achieve superior performance over modified architectures. Chronos-2 shows competitive zero-shot performance on two datasets but fails on TSO special events. Key findings: importance of long input contexts, covariates, and continuous retraining.
AI.conclusion: Standard Transformer architectures are most effective for load forecasting across grid levels, challenging the need for architectural modifications. The research provides valuable benchmarking insights and highlights often-overlooked practical aspects like input context length and continuous retraining.

title: CardioMeta: Calibrated Multi-Task Prediction of Diabetes, Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Disease Across Population and EHR Data
summary: Cardiometabolic diseases remain among the most persistent drivers of preventable morbidity because diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease frequently co-occur and share metabolic, vascular, demographic, and behavioral determinants. Existing machine learning studies for chronic disease prediction often emphasize discrimination on a single dataset, while underreporting label leakage, calibration, temporal robustness, external transportability, and subgroup reliability. This paper presents CardioMeta, a calibrated multi-task framework for joint prediction of diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease across population survey and electronic health record (EHR) data. The study uses NHANES for population-level model development and temporal validation, and MIMIC-IV for EHR-domain evaluation under substantial distribution shift. To reduce circular label reconstruction, the primary analysis excludes disease-defining variables from the corresponding prediction heads, while a full-clinical feature setting is retained only as sensitivity analysis. CardioMeta combines a shared cardiometabolic encoder with disease-specific gated heads and post-hoc probability calibration. In the leakage-reduced temporal validation setting, the model achieved a macro-AUROC of 0.839, macro-AUPRC of 0.536, macro-F1 of 0.614, and expected calibration error of 0.024, with modest but consistent improvements over strong gradient-boosting and neural tabular baselines. External evaluation on MIMIC-IV showed clear degradation under domain shift, while limited fine-tuning partially recovered performance. The findings indicate that the principal value of multi-task cardiometabolic modeling lies not in inflated accuracy, but in reproducible leakage control, calibrated probabilities, and transparent reliability reporting across heterogeneous healthcare data sources.

AI.tldr: CardioMeta is a calibrated multi-task framework for joint prediction of diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, designed to address common ML pitfalls like label leakage and poor calibration while ensuring reliability across different healthcare datasets.
AI.motivation: Cardiometabolic diseases are major causes of preventable morbidity that frequently co-occur and share common determinants. Existing ML studies for chronic disease prediction often focus on single-dataset discrimination while neglecting critical issues like label leakage, calibration, temporal robustness, external transportability, and subgroup reliability.
AI.method: CardioMeta uses a multi-task framework combining a shared cardiometabolic encoder with disease-specific gated heads and post-hoc probability calibration. It's developed using NHANES for population-level modeling and temporal validation, and evaluated on MIMIC-IV EHR data under distribution shift. The primary analysis excludes disease-defining variables from prediction heads to prevent label leakage, with full clinical features only used in sensitivity analysis.
AI.result: In leakage-reduced temporal validation: macro-AUROC of 0.839, macro-AUPRC of 0.536, macro-F1 of 0.614, and expected calibration error of 0.024, with modest but consistent improvements over strong baselines. External evaluation on MIMIC-IV showed performance degradation under domain shift, though limited fine-tuning partially recovered performance.
AI.conclusion: The main value of multi-task cardiometabolic modeling lies not in inflated accuracy, but in reproducible leakage control, calibrated probabilities, and transparent reliability reporting across heterogeneous healthcare data sources.

title: Learning Faster without Deeper Networks: A*-Inspired Batch Selection for Efficient CNN Training
summary: Common practice when training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) is to use randomly shuffled mini-batches. This creates two limitations: slower convergence, and a diminishing learning signal, since many samples are quickly classified as easy during training. We address these inefficiencies with A*-Inspired Batch Selection (A*-BS), a lightweight, model-agnostic strategy that formulates mini-batch scheduling as a heuristic search problem. Each batch is treated as a node in a search space and ranked using an A*-like score combining a loss-based difficulty measure with a reuse penalty. This encourages informative gradient updates and batch diversity throughout training, without modifying network architectures or optimization algorithms, so it integrates seamlessly into existing pipelines. We evaluate A*-BS on the twelve 2D classification tasks of the MedMNIST-v2 benchmark, using a deliberately simple architecture of approximately 2.25x10^5 parameters, compared against the ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 baselines reported by the benchmark. On half of these tasks, the lightweight model with A*-BS reaches higher accuracy and AUC than both ResNet baselines, with relative gains of up to 15%. An ablation under identical architecture and hyperparameters shows A*-BS outperforms random batch shuffling on all twelve tasks. Wall-clock measurements further show the lightweight CNN with A*-BS trains substantially faster than ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 on identical hardware. These results indicate that intelligent batch ordering can partially compensate for reduced architectural complexity, offering a computationally efficient alternative to deeper models, with reliability reinforced by strong performance even against deeper, more sophisticated architectures.

AI.tldr: A*-Inspired Batch Selection (A*-BS) improves CNN training by using intelligent batch ordering instead of random shuffling, achieving faster convergence and better performance with simpler models.
AI.motivation: Random mini-batch shuffling in CNN training has two main limitations: slower convergence and diminishing learning signal as many easy samples are quickly learned. This creates inefficiencies in the training process.
AI.method: A*-BS formulates mini-batch scheduling as a heuristic search problem where each batch is a node ranked using an A*-like score combining loss-based difficulty measure with a reuse penalty. This encourages informative gradient updates and batch diversity without modifying network architectures or optimization algorithms.
AI.result: On 6 out of 12 MedMNIST-v2 tasks, a lightweight model (225k parameters) with A*-BS outperformed ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 baselines with relative gains up to 15%. A*-BS beat random shuffling on all 12 tasks in ablation studies and trained substantially faster than ResNets on identical hardware.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent batch ordering can partially compensate for reduced architectural complexity, offering a computationally efficient alternative to deeper models. The method's reliability is reinforced by strong performance even against more sophisticated architectures.

title: Trainable Spline Representations for Physics-Informed Learning
summary: This work introduces Physics-Informed Splines (PI-Splines), a structured spline-based architecture for physics-informed learning. Instead of representing the solution of a differential equation with a neural network, PI-Splines directly parametrize the unknown field through a tensor-product B-spline expansion with trainable control coefficients. This formulation preserves the residual-based training paradigm of Physics-Informed Neural Networks while providing compact support, explicit smoothness control, analytical derivatives, and a direct geometric interpretation of the trainable parameters. When compatible with the spline representation, boundary conditions can be imposed strongly by fixing suitable boundary control coefficients. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark problems of increasing difficulty and compared with standard physics-informed frameworks under matched governing equations, collocation sets, loss terms, and optimization procedures, so as to isolate the effect of the approximation architecture. Numerical experiments show that PI-Splines provide a competitive and stable alternative to neural physics-informed architectures, particularly in settings where structured representations, locality, and parameter efficiency are desirable.

AI.tldr: PI-Splines replaces neural networks with tensor-product B-splines for physics-informed learning, offering structured representation with compact support, analytical derivatives, and strong boundary condition enforcement.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) by providing a more structured, interpretable alternative with explicit smoothness control, locality, and parameter efficiency while preserving the residual-based training paradigm.
AI.method: Uses tensor-product B-spline expansion with trainable control coefficients to parametrize unknown fields in differential equations. Enables strong imposition of boundary conditions by fixing boundary control coefficients when compatible with spline representation.
AI.result: PI-Splines demonstrate competitive and stable performance compared to standard physics-informed neural architectures across benchmark problems of increasing difficulty, particularly excelling in settings requiring structured representations, locality, and parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PI-Splines provide a viable structured alternative to neural physics-informed architectures, offering advantages in interpretability, computational stability, and parameter efficiency while maintaining the flexibility of residual-based training.

title: CoG-Guided Weight Correction for Fault-Tolerant Deep Neural Networks
comment: 7 figures, 9 tables. the paper is under review in an IEEE Transactions
summary:
Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) used in safety-critical applications are vulnerable to hardware and memory faults that corrupt network weights and degrade reliability. In this paper, we propose a Center of Gravity (CoG) guided weight correction method that restores faulty weights based on their spatial characteristics within each layer. The proposed approach detects and corrects weight faults using distance-aware correction rules, eliminating the need for retraining or architectural modification. The effectiveness of the proposed method in terms of the capability of tolerating hardware faults has been evaluated through performing fault injection at different Bit Error Rates (BERs).
  Experiments on safety-critical LSTM-based Networks, including StageNet for disease progression tracking and MTFNet for cardiac anomaly detection, demonstrate fault tolerance improvements of up to 230x and 6.41x, respectively, at a BER of 10^{-3}, with negligible accuracy loss. When extended to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), the method achieves up to 49.55x and 20.79x improvements under comparable fault conditions on ResNet-18 and VGG-16, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to apply the CoG concept to neural network weight tensors for enhancing model reliability.

AI.tldr: CoG-guided weight correction method detects and corrects hardware faults in DNN weights using spatial characteristics without retraining, achieving significant fault tolerance improvements in LSTMs and CNNs with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Deep Neural Networks in safety-critical applications are vulnerable to hardware and memory faults that corrupt network weights, degrading reliability and posing safety risks.
AI.method: Proposes a Center of Gravity (CoG) guided weight correction method that detects and corrects faulty weights based on spatial characteristics within each layer using distance-aware correction rules, eliminating the need for retraining or architectural modifications.
AI.result: Achieved fault tolerance improvements of up to 230x and 6.41x in LSTM networks (StageNet and MTFNet) at BER 10^-3 with negligible accuracy loss. For CNNs, achieved up to 49.55x and 20.79x improvements in ResNet-18 and VGG-16 under comparable fault conditions.
AI.conclusion: The CoG-guided approach effectively enhances DNN reliability against hardware faults without retraining, with applications in safety-critical systems. This is the first work applying CoG concept to neural network weight tensors for reliability enhancement.

title: From Diffusion to Reaction-Diffusion: A Dynamical-Systems View of Oversmoothing in Hypergraph Neural Networks
summary: Higher-order couplings enhance the expressive power of hypergraph neural networks (HGNNs), but they also intensify representation collapse in deep propagation due to strong multi-way feature mixing. This work investigates hypergraph oversmoothing from a dynamical-systems perspective and develops a reaction--diffusion framework for depth-resistant hypergraph learning. By defining hypergraph gradient and divergence operators, we interpret message passing as an incidence-level diffusion process. The analysis of pure diffusion shows that its continuous semiflow exponentially contracts the null-mode-free component of node representations and drives the Dirichlet energy to zero, revealing hypergraph oversmoothing as an intrinsic transverse-energy dissipation phenomenon. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Hypergraph Neural Reaction--Diffusion (HNRD), which introduces a reaction mechanism acting on the transverse component to compensate diffusion-induced dissipation and stabilize discriminative variations. We establish global well-posedness of the proposed dynamics and prove that the null-mode-free Dirichlet energy remains bounded away from zero. A forward-Euler discretization provides a practical HNRD layer with a stability condition for deep propagation. Experiments on benchmark and synthetic heterophilic hypergraphs demonstrate that HNRD consistently improves over representative hypergraph baselines. Depth, robustness, and efficiency analyses further show that HNRD preserves stable performance and nonzero Dirichlet energy under deep propagation and perturbations. These results provide a principled dynamical framework for designing deep hypergraph architectures that maintain higher-order expressiveness without representation collapse.

AI.tldr: HNRD introduces a reaction-diffusion framework for deep hypergraph learning that prevents representation collapse by compensating diffusion-induced dissipation, enabling stable deep propagation while preserving higher-order expressiveness.
AI.motivation: Higher-order couplings in hypergraph neural networks enhance expressive power but cause representation collapse in deep propagation due to strong multi-way feature mixing. This oversmoothing problem needs to be addressed to enable deep hypergraph learning architectures.
AI.method: Develops Hypergraph Neural Reaction-Diffusion (HNRD) framework by defining hypergraph gradient/divergence operators to interpret message passing as diffusion. Adds reaction mechanism acting on transverse component to compensate diffusion-induced dissipation. Uses forward-Euler discretization with stability condition for practical implementation.
AI.result: HNRD consistently outperforms baseline methods on benchmark and synthetic heterophilic hypergraphs. Maintains stable performance and nonzero Dirichlet energy under deep propagation and perturbations. Proves global well-posedness of dynamics and bounded null-mode-free Dirichlet energy away from zero.
AI.conclusion: The reaction-diffusion framework provides a principled dynamical approach for designing deep hypergraph architectures that maintain higher-order expressiveness without representation collapse, offering theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements.

title: Scaling Time Series Classification via XAI-Driven Data Reduction
comment: Accepted for AALTD workshop at ECML-PKDD 2026
summary:
Explainable AI (XAI) for time series has seen significant algorithmic growth, but its utility in providing measurable performance gains for downstream tasks remains under-explored. This paper bridges this gap by introducing drXAI, a novel methodology that repurposes XAI attribution methods for effective data reduction in Time Series Classification (TSC). The core challenge in modern TSC is scalability; state-of-the-art models, such as Transformers, exhibit quadratic complexity relative to sequence length and linear complexity relative to the number of channels. This renders them computationally prohibitive for massive datasets. drXAI addresses this by using a fast, GPU-accelerated classifier (Hydra) to generate local attributions. We aggregate these into global feature importance scores and employ an automated elbow-cut heuristic to select the most salient features without requiring manual thresholds.
  We evaluate our approach on both synthetic and real-world univariate and multivariate datasets. On synthetic benchmarks, drXAI successfully recovers ground-truth features where traditional baselines fail. On real-world data, drXAI achieves between 80% and 90% data reduction while maintaining classification accuracy comparable to models trained on the full dataset. Most importantly, we show that drXAI allows resource-intensive models like ConvTran to scale to datasets that were previously inaccessible due to memory constraints. Our results show the benefits of using XAI not just for interpretability, but as a robust tool for feature selection and scalability in time series analysis. All our code and data are openly available.

AI.tldr: drXAI is a novel XAI methodology that repurposes attribution methods for data reduction in time series classification, enabling scalability of computationally intensive models while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern time series classification models (like Transformers) have quadratic complexity relative to sequence length and linear complexity relative to channels, making them computationally prohibitive for massive datasets. XAI for time series has grown algorithmically but its utility in providing measurable performance gains for downstream tasks remains under-explored.
AI.method: drXAI uses a fast GPU-accelerated classifier (Hydra) to generate local attributions, aggregates these into global feature importance scores, and employs an automated elbow-cut heuristic to select the most salient features without requiring manual thresholds for effective data reduction.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks, drXAI successfully recovers ground-truth features where traditional baselines fail. On real-world data, it achieves 80-90% data reduction while maintaining comparable classification accuracy. It enables resource-intensive models like ConvTran to scale to previously inaccessible datasets due to memory constraints.
AI.conclusion: XAI can be used not just for interpretability, but as a robust tool for feature selection and scalability in time series analysis. drXAI bridges the gap between XAI algorithmic growth and measurable performance gains for downstream tasks in time series classification.

title: AquaAugmentor: A Novel Feature Augmentation Algorithm for Water Potability Prediction
comment: Published in 2024 6th International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Industry 5.0 (STI), 14-15 December, Dhaka, Bangladesh
summary: Access to potable water is crucial for health, economic development, and sustainability. However, accurately classifying water quality remains a significant challenge due to the complexity and variability of water source data. This paper addresses the challenge of predicting water potability through machine learning and deep learning algorithms. It introduces a novel feature augmentation algorithm, AquaAugmentor, to enhance the predictive performance of these models for low-dimensional datasets. Utilizing a dataset that includes chemical attributes of water, such as pH, hardness, solids, chloramines, sulfate, and others. This study evaluates the performance of the models with and without AquaAugmentor. Each model applied to classify water as potable or non-potable and its performance is then evaluated and compared based on test accuracy and AUC score. The results highlight the strengths and limitations of our proposed algorithm, providing insights into the most effective techniques for improving the predictive performance of water quality classification. This study contributes to the broader efforts of ensuring safe water access and serves as a framework for employing machine learning in environmental quality assessments. The findings aim to assist researchers, policymakers, and public health officials in making informed decisions based on reliable machine learning predictions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes AquaAugmentor, a novel feature augmentation algorithm to enhance ML/DL models for water potability classification, particularly effective for low-dimensional datasets.
AI.motivation: Accurate water quality classification is crucial for health and sustainability, but remains challenging due to complex and variable water source data, especially with low-dimensional datasets where model performance can be limited.
AI.method: Introduces AquaAugmentor for feature augmentation; applies ML/DL algorithms to water quality dataset with chemical attributes (pH, hardness, solids, etc.); evaluates models with/without augmentation using test accuracy and AUC scores for potability classification.
AI.result: The study demonstrates improved predictive performance using AquaAugmentor, highlighting strengths/limitations of the algorithm and providing insights into effective techniques for water quality classification enhancement.
AI.conclusion: AquaAugmentor effectively improves water potability classification, contributes to safe water access efforts, provides a framework for ML in environmental assessment, and offers practical guidance for researchers and policymakers.

title: Knowledge-Assisted Multi-Graph Dependency Learning for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection in Multi-Stage Industrial Processes
summary: Industrial processes often generate complex, interdependent time-series data from multiple sensors across multiple stages, forming complex dependencies among variables and process stages. Effective monitoring and timely anomaly detection of these time series through multivariate time series anomaly detection (MTAD) is crucial for preventing failures and ensuring the reliability of automated systems. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have advanced MTAD by leveraging data-driven graphs to model complex dependencies among variables, effectively capturing relational structures within multivariate time series to enhance anomaly detection performance. However, existing GNN-based approaches often overlook critical process knowledge, and even when this knowledge is considered, seamlessly incorporating it into existing models remains inherently challenging, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose a knowledge-assisted multi-graph framework for modeling sensor dependencies in multi-stage industrial processes for MTAD, which explicitly incorporates process knowledge into graph learning to enhance dependency modeling and improve anomaly detection performance. Our method constructs three complementary graphs: one purely data-driven and two refined by integrating structural constraints derived from process knowledge. To effectively leverage these graphs for anomaly detection, we employ a multi-graph attention network, enabling a more accurate and robust representation of complex dependencies. Comprehensive experiments on two real-world, multi-stage industrial datasets demonstrate that incorporating process knowledge substantially enhances anomaly detection performance.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-assisted multi-graph framework for multivariate time series anomaly detection that integrates process knowledge with data-driven graphs to better model sensor dependencies in multi-stage industrial processes.
AI.motivation: Industrial processes generate complex, interdependent time-series data from multiple sensors across multiple stages. Existing GNN-based MTAD approaches often overlook critical process knowledge or struggle to incorporate it effectively, leading to suboptimal anomaly detection performance despite GNNs' ability to model complex dependencies.
AI.method: Proposes a knowledge-assisted multi-graph framework that constructs three complementary graphs: one purely data-driven and two refined by integrating structural constraints derived from process knowledge. Uses a multi-graph attention network to effectively leverage these graphs for anomaly detection, enabling more accurate representation of complex dependencies.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on two real-world, multi-stage industrial datasets demonstrate that incorporating process knowledge substantially enhances anomaly detection performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly incorporating process knowledge into graph learning through a multi-graph framework significantly improves dependency modeling and anomaly detection performance in multi-stage industrial processes, addressing limitations of purely data-driven GNN approaches.

title: QUADS: Stabilizing NVFP4 Reinforcement Learning for MoE via QUantization-error Alignment across Dual Sides
summary: Rollout generation is a major bottleneck in Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Large Language Models, motivating low-precision rollout acceleration such as FP8. As an emerging low-precision format, NVFP4 combines fine-grained scaling for accuracy preservation with native W4A4 FP4 GEMMs for higher throughput than FP8. However, we find that directly applying NVFP4 to MoE RL rollout is impractical. NVFP4 rollout with BF16 training collapses after roughly 150 steps, accompanied by rapidly growing rollout-trainer log-probability gaps. Through training-inference error analysis and controlled ablations, we identify activation error, rather than weight error, as the dominant source of FP4 RL instability: weights can be synchronized and aligned by a shared quantization-dequantization path, whereas activations are recomputed online and error is amplified by the coarse E2M1 grid. Therefore, to stabilize NVFP4 RL for MoE, we propose QUantization-error Alignment across Dual Sides (QUADS). On the trainer side, we introduce Asymmetric Quantization-Aware Training fake-quantizing weights while keeping activations unquantized for better alignment. On the rollout side, Residual Activation Compensation corrects high-error activation channels while preserving native W4A4 GEMMs. In our MoE RL experiments on several benchmarks, QUADS achieves BF16-level accuracy, improves average pass@1 by 21.49 points over naive NVFP4 RL, and delivers ~16% higher rollout throughput than FP8.

AI.tldr: QUADS enables stable FP4 reinforcement learning for MoE LLMs by aligning quantization errors across training and rollout sides, achieving BF16-level accuracy with higher throughput than FP8.
AI.motivation: Rollout generation is a major bottleneck in RL for MoE LLMs, motivating low-precision acceleration like FP8. While NVFP4 offers better throughput than FP8, it causes RL instability when directly applied to MoE rollouts, with training collapsing after ~150 steps due to activation errors.
AI.method: Proposes QUantization-error Alignment across Dual Sides (QUADS): On trainer side, uses Asymmetric Quantization-Aware Training (fake-quantizing weights while keeping activations unquantized). On rollout side, employs Residual Activation Compensation to correct high-error activation channels while preserving native W4A4 GEMMs.
AI.result: QUADS achieves BF16-level accuracy in MoE RL experiments, improves average pass@1 by 21.49 points over naive NVFP4 RL, and delivers ~16% higher rollout throughput than FP8.
AI.conclusion: QUADS successfully stabilizes NVFP4 RL for MoE LLMs by addressing the core issue of activation errors through dual-side alignment, making FP4 rollout acceleration practical with maintained accuracy and improved throughput.

title: In-context learning of closed form solution to simple linear regression task using transformer with linear self-attention
summary: In-context learning is a remarkable property of transformers and has recently received a lot of interest. In many studies of in-context learning, it has been shown that transformers are capable of implementing solver for linear and non-linear regression problems, in which the most of them implement gradient descent algorithm. However, it is still unclear whether those implementations have actually been acquired through training. In this paper, we construct a transformer with linear self-attention, which in-context learns the least squares estimate in a simple regression task. The point here is that the closed form (analytical) solution is approximately obtained by using layer normalization rather than an approximate solution based on gradient descent algorithm. Then, we show an experimental example, in which our implementation is mainly used in the transformer trained with l1 regularization when the target output is the least squares estimate.

AI.tldr: Transformers can learn least squares regression through in-context learning, but it's unclear if this is acquired through training. The paper shows transformers with linear self-attention can obtain analytical least squares solutions via layer normalization, not gradient descent approximations.
AI.motivation: While transformers demonstrate impressive in-context learning capabilities for regression problems, most implementations use gradient descent approximations. There's uncertainty about whether these capabilities are actually learned during training. The authors want to explore whether transformers can implement analytical (closed-form) solutions rather than iterative gradient descent approximations.
AI.method: Constructed a transformer with linear self-attention layers. Used layer normalization to enable the model to approximate the analytical closed-form solution for least squares regression. Tested this approach in experimental settings, particularly when training with L1 regularization and targeting least squares estimates.
AI.result: The transformer with linear self-attention and layer normalization can in-context learn the least squares estimate, obtaining the analytical solution rather than gradient descent approximations. This implementation is mainly observed when training with L1 regularization and when the target output is the least squares estimate.
AI.conclusion: Transformers can implement analytical solutions to regression problems through in-context learning, particularly when using specific architectural choices like linear self-attention and layer normalization. This contrasts with previous findings that transformers mainly implement gradient descent approximations, suggesting more direct mathematical implementations are possible.

title: Graph Coloring Approach to Solving Sudoku with Oscillatory Neural Networks
summary: Oscillatory Neural Networks (ONNs) present an attractive physics-based computing paradigm rooted in the dynamics of a network of typically fully coupled oscillators aiming to minimize an underlying energy function. In this paper, we propose an ONN-based solver for one well-known constrained combinatorial optimization problem, namely a Sudoku, by formulating the problem as a Graph Coloring problem. By modifying the already existing Graph Coloring solver to a computationally cheaper version and introducing an additional term ensuring the fulfillment of the Sudoku constraints, our solver is shown to significantly outperform the existing HNN- and ONN solvers in terms of accuracy. In particular, we are able to achieve nearly flawless accuracies on $4 \times 4$ as well as rather high accuracies on $9 \times 9$ Sudoku puzzles for different numbers of unknown digits.

AI.tldr: An oscillatory neural network approach for solving Sudoku by formulating it as a graph coloring problem, achieving higher accuracy than existing HNN and ONN solvers through computational optimizations and constraint enforcement.
AI.motivation: Oscillatory Neural Networks offer a physics-based computing paradigm for optimization problems, but existing solvers for constrained combinatorial problems like Sudoku need improvement in accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Formulate Sudoku as a graph coloring problem, modify existing Graph Coloring solver to a computationally cheaper version, and introduce additional terms to ensure Sudoku constraints are satisfied.
AI.result: The proposed solver significantly outperforms existing HNN- and ONN-based solvers in accuracy, achieving nearly flawless results on 4×4 Sudoku puzzles and high accuracies on 9×9 puzzles with different numbers of unknown digits.
AI.conclusion: ONN-based approach effectively solves Sudoku puzzles by leveraging graph coloring formulation with constraint enforcement, demonstrating superior performance over previous methods and validating the approach for constrained combinatorial optimization problems.

title: On the Failure of Boundary-Seeking Distillation in Bottlenecked Generative Architectures
summary: Data-free knowledge distillation transfers the knowledge encoded in a teacher model to a student model without access to the original training data. Prior work such as Contrastive Abductive Knowledge Extraction (CAKE) achieves this for classifiers by synthesizing samples near the teacher's decision boundary. In this work, we investigate whether this boundary-seeking principle extends to autoencoder distillation through experiments on the MNIST dataset . To enable a direct comparison, we reformulate continuous reconstruction as a dense, per-feature classification task, allowing the decoder to output categorical logits. We show that boundary-seeking objectives are fundamentally ill-posed in bottlenecked generative architectures. CAKE operates on a single, instance-level objective, but a decoder acts as an array of tightly coupled, feature-level classifiers constrained by a shared low-dimensional bottleneck. Independently sampling contrastive targets for these coupled outputs violates the geometry of the learned latent manifold and produces severe gradient conflicts instead of informative boundary samples. Manifold-aware synthesis bypasses these conflicts entirely and establishes an effective baseline for data-free generative distillation.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that boundary-seeking data-free distillation methods like CAKE don't work well for autoencoders because decoder outputs are coupled feature-level classifiers constrained by a shared bottleneck, causing gradient conflicts when sampled independently. Manifold-aware synthesis provides a better baseline.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether boundary-seeking principles from classifier distillation (like CAKE) extend to autoencoder distillation, since prior work focused on classifiers but autoencoders are important for generative models.
AI.method: Reformulate continuous reconstruction as dense per-feature classification to enable comparison, analyze why boundary-seeking fails for bottlenecked architectures, and propose manifold-aware synthesis as an alternative baseline.
AI.result: Boundary-seeking objectives are fundamentally ill-posed for bottlenecked generative architectures due to coupled feature-level classifiers constrained by shared bottlenecks, causing severe gradient conflicts rather than informative boundary samples.
AI.conclusion: Manifold-aware synthesis bypasses these conflicts and establishes an effective baseline for data-free generative distillation, showing that classifier distillation principles don't directly translate to autoencoder architectures.

title: Data-Native Global Optimization for Big Data K-means Clustering
summary: Big data clustering remains challenging: the Minimum Sum-of-Squares Clustering (MSSC) problem underlying K-means is NP-hard, and existing methods either reach poor local minima or require prohibitive metaheuristic hybrids. We target arbitrarily tall data: a fixed feature space may contain arbitrarily many, possibly infinitely many, observations, while the algorithm accesses only finite random samples. We propose Big-means++, an algorithm achieving scalability and global-search quality by curating inputs to MSSC optimization on big data. It orchestrates local K-means refinements into a data-native global search for big data clustering. Rather than optimizing the full-data MSSC objective, Big-means++ traverses sample-induced surrogate landscapes. Each sample defines a distinct empirical MSSC approximation with a perturbed local-optimum structure, turning sample-to-sample variation into a global-search mechanism. Unlike Big-means, a flowing-incumbent strategy propagates centroid state across empirical landscapes through K-means refinements on fresh samples without rollback to a best-so-far solution. This increases mobility and favors stable, high-quality configurations across approximations of the full-data structure. A new shaking mechanism varies sample size geometrically, broadening the surrogate landscapes explored across resolution scales, accounting for cluster imbalance, and improving solution quality. A competitive multi-agent system asynchronously explores independent sampled landscapes, transforming diverse stochastic trajectories into collective search intelligence. Automatic convergence detection stops each agent after attaining a high-quality solution but before further search risks degrading it, while providing a universal speed-quality control. Experiments on 22 datasets against 11 competing algorithms demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness of Big-means++.

AI.tldr: Big-means++ is a scalable K-means algorithm for big data that uses sample-based surrogate landscapes and a multi-agent system to achieve global-search quality while maintaining efficiency.
AI.motivation: The MSSC problem underlying K-means is NP-hard, and existing methods either reach poor local minima or require prohibitive metaheuristic hybrids. There's a need for scalable algorithms that can handle arbitrarily tall data (many observations in fixed feature space) while accessing only finite random samples.
AI.method: Big-means++ orchestrates local K-means refinements into a global search using sample-induced surrogate landscapes. It employs: 1) flowing-incumbent strategy that propagates centroids across samples without rollback, 2) geometric sample-size variation (shaking) for exploring different resolution scales, 3) competitive multi-agent system exploring independent sampled landscapes asynchronously, and 4) automatic convergence detection.
AI.result: Experiments on 22 datasets against 11 competing algorithms demonstrate that Big-means++ achieves effective, efficient, and robust clustering performance. The method successfully balances scalability with global-search quality.
AI.conclusion: Big-means++ provides a novel approach to big data clustering that transforms sample-to-sample variation into a global-search mechanism, enabling scalable optimization of MSSC problems while maintaining solution quality through intelligent exploration of surrogate landscapes.

title: Revisiting data-driven dynamic security assessment with a tabular foundation model
comment: Paper is in the review process
summary: Data-driven pre-fault dynamic security assessment (DSA) rapidly evaluates the dynamic risk of credible contingencies on a power system using machine learning. Existing approaches face two limitations. First, they require a large labelled database for training, with a separate model trained, tuned, and maintained for each contingency in a potentially long list of credible contingencies. Second, the trained models generalize poorly to unseen contingencies. This work addresses the limitations by using a tabular foundation model (TFM) that assesses stability through in-context learning, requiring no retraining or hyperparameter optimization. A single TFM can assess many contingencies at once, removing the need for one model per classifier. We also characterize when the use of electrical distance coordinates (EDC) as continuous features enables generalization of TFM to unseen contingencies and when they do not, demonstrating how a few labelled samples can reliably improve generalization. Through comprehensive case studies on the IEEE 68-bus system, we show that a single TFM attains an average Macro F1 score of about 90% with only 120 labelled samples per contingency, roughly two orders of magnitude fewer than conventionally assumed, without any model retraining or hyperparameter tuning. For new/unseen contingencies, we show that using just 10 labelled samples of the new contingency with EDC encoding matches the best achievable transfer learning oracle model, which requires fully labelled data and is not deployable in practice. Overall, this initial study paves the way towards developing and deploying foundation models for power system operations, with possible applications across multiple operational tasks.

AI.tldr: A tabular foundation model enables dynamic security assessment with minimal labeled data, addressing generalization to unseen contingencies through in-context learning without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven DSA approaches require large labeled databases and separate models for each contingency, with poor generalization to unseen contingencies.
AI.method: Uses a tabular foundation model that assesses stability through in-context learning, requiring no retraining or hyperparameter optimization, and employs electrical distance coordinates to enable generalization.
AI.result: A single TFM achieves ~90% Macro F1 score with only 120 labeled samples per contingency (2 orders of magnitude fewer than conventional methods), and with just 10 samples of new contingencies using EDC encoding matches best transfer learning performance.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates foundation models' potential for power system operations, enabling efficient DSA with minimal data and good generalization across multiple operational tasks.

title: ContinuityBench: A Benchmark and Systems Study of Stateful Failover in Multi-Provider LLM Routing
summary: In production large language model (LLM) deployments, high API availability guarantees do not equate to conversational continuity. When a primary provider experiences an outage or strict rate-limiting, naive stateless failover mechanisms successfully maintain uptime but silently discard conversation history, severely disrupting the user experience. To rigorously quantify and resolve this failure mode, we introduce two novel metrics: Continuity Preservation Rate (CPR) and Continuity Latency Overhead (CLO). We propose a stateful, multi-provider proxy architecture utilizing a History-Forwarding strategy to seamlessly reconstruct conversational state across heterogeneous LLM endpoints during failover events. Furthermore, we release continuity-bench, https://github.com/Vishal-sys-code/continuity-bench, an open evaluation harness designed to stress-test context preservation under high-concurrency provider failure conditions. Our empirical evaluation ($N=750$ failover events) demonstrates that our stateful proxy achieves a 99.20\% CPR [95\% CI: 98.27\%, 99.63\%], cleanly transferring deep conversational context to fallback providers, compared to a near-0\% preservation rate for standard stateless architectures. Finally, we characterize failover latency distributions, identifying the critical necessity of asynchronous exponential backoff with jitter to prevent cascading retry storms against strict-limit fallback APIs. Our results provide a principled foundation for building robust, state-preserving multi-model inference systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a stateful proxy architecture with history-forwarding to maintain conversational continuity during LLM provider failover events, introducing metrics (CPR, CLO) and releasing an evaluation benchmark (continuity-bench).
AI.motivation: Current LLM deployments have high API availability but lack conversational continuity during provider outages or rate-limiting. Stateless failover mechanisms maintain uptime but silently discard conversation history, severely disrupting user experience.
AI.method: Proposed a stateful, multi-provider proxy architecture using a History-Forwarding strategy to reconstruct conversational state across heterogeneous LLM endpoints during failover. Introduced metrics CPR (Continuity Preservation Rate) and CLO (Continuity Latency Overhead), and released continuity-bench evaluation harness for stress-testing context preservation under high-concurrency failure conditions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation (N=750 failover events) shows stateful proxy achieves 99.20% CPR [95% CI: 98.27%, 99.63%], cleanly transferring deep conversational context to fallback providers, compared to near-0% for stateless architectures. Characterized failover latency distributions and identified necessity of asynchronous exponential backoff with jitter to prevent cascading retry storms.
AI.conclusion: The stateful proxy architecture provides a principled foundation for building robust, state-preserving multi-model inference systems that maintain conversational continuity during provider failures, addressing a critical gap in current LLM deployment practices.

title: When Model Merging Rivals Joint Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning: A Task-Vector Geometry Analysis
